text.title: AI Coding Tool Selection and Combination
text.summary: Match AI coding tools to task type: Claude Code for long-context planning and refactors, Cursor for flow-state implementation, Copilot for inline boilerplate. Combination use produces better output than any single tool.

rule.statement: Select the AI coding tool based on the task type, not personal preference. Mismatching tool to task produces inferior output.
rule.rationale: Each tool optimizes for a different workflow: Claude Code has no IDE dependency and handles long context; Cursor provides real-time feedback during coding; Copilot is optimized for inline speed. Forcing one tool to do another's job wastes its strengths.

list.item: Claude Code: documentation, test suites, large refactors, complex reasoning, terminal-based tasks, agentic pipelines.
Cursor: exploratory feature implementation, flow-state coding, multi-file edits with real-time feedback, quick changes with visual context.
Copilot: daily coding acceleration, boilerplate generation, inline completion, repetitive patterns.

rule.statement: Use tools sequentially for a single feature: Claude Code for research and planning, Cursor for implementation, Copilot for inline completion during implementation.
rule.rationale: Context fragmentation occurs when multiple tools operate on the same feature simultaneously — each tool sees different history. Sequential use maintains context coherence at each stage.

example.note: Sequential multi-tool workflow for a feature.
example.code: Research/Planning → Claude Code (long context, no IDE) Implementation → Cursor (real-time feedback, multi-file) Inline completion → Copilot (speed, boilerplate)

rule.statement: Always review AI-generated code before accepting. Never auto-accept security-critical code (auth, payment, data access, business logic).
rule.rationale: AI coding tools increase defect rate 4x when used without review discipline (per 2025 research). AI tools trained on public data reproduce patterns from outdated library versions. Static analysis (ruff, Semgrep, Bandit) must run in CI regardless of AI involvement.

rule.statement: Provide structured context in every AI prompt: tech stack, constraints, expected output format. Vague prompts produce vague code.
rule.rationale: AI tools without architecture context infer project structure from limited file access. Vague prompts produce generic patterns that require significant rework to fit the actual codebase.

text.reference: Structured prompt template for code generation tasks
